items.title: THE SECOND | Animatic vs. Final (Bonus Video)
items.author: Every Frame a Painting
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>A side-by-side comparison of the animatic for “The Second” and the final film, with commentary provided by Taylor Ramos & Tony Zhou. Please enjoy, and we’ll see you for the next project.

Storyboarded by Taylor Ramos
Edited by Tony Zhou

Chapters
00:00 Introduction
01:08 Commentary Start, Realities of Production
02:09 Project Origins, Barry Lyndon, Creating Animatic
03:07 Working Process with Animatic
03:50 Green Screen PMP, 2nd Unit Reshoots
04:52 Zorro masks, long lenses, compromising with locations
06:07 Compressing multiple set-ups into one
06:59 Splitting up locations to break up movie visually
07:30 Duel negotiations
08:21 The Back to Back shot
09:01 Taylor’s plexiglass drawovers on set
09:33 Paul and Ethan in the argument scene
10:38 Composer, music cues
11:09 Tony’s composition vs. Taylor’s composition
11:31 Yes it was a real cloud passing his face
12:00 Painterly sequence, gun montage, VFX sky replacements
13:00 Subjectivity, no dialogue at beginning or end
13:50 Directing Ethan & Colton for the duel
14:40 Alfonso's moments of "ma," Taylor's first drawing
15:31 Deleted Shots & Scenes & Thank Yous

Links to some of the clips
The Rescuers (1977) story session: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=flLu5iDVI7c
Zootopia (2016) deleted scene: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MnH46nq8gao
The Grand Budapest Hotel (2014) animatic clip: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3rcVFEgCaOM

You can check out some merch we made here:
https://store.dftba.com/collections/every-frame-a-painting</body></html>

items.title: THE SECOND | Short Film
items.author: Every Frame a Painting
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>In an alternate version of today’s world where dueling is still acceptable, Philip, a man of tradition, must perform the role of “Second” on the day of his only son’s duel.

“The Second,” starring Paul Sun-Hyung Lee (Kim’s Convenience, The Mandalorian, Avatar: The Last Airbender) and Ethan Hwang (Riceboy Sleeps, Umbrella Academy) is the first live-action narrative short written & directed by Taylor Ramos and Tony Zhou. It premiered at Fantasia in 2024.

Credits:
Starring: Paul Sun-Hyung Lee, Ethan Hwang, Aaron Schwartz, Richard Wes, Colton Royce, Martin Ortega, Sam Osei, Tyrus Hwang

Written & Directed by: Taylor Ramos & Tony Zhou
Produced by: Nach Dudsdeemaytha, Jon Warne, Eric Bizzarri
Associate Producer: Rebeca Ortiz
Director of Photography: Alfonso Chin
Production Designer: Justin Campbell
Costume Designer: Asoomii Jay
Music by: Sean William
Sound Designer: May Guimarães
Storyboarded by: Taylor Ramos
Edited by: Tony Zhou
Casting by: Maureen Webb & Dylan Rogers</body></html>

items.title: Where Do You Put the Camera?
items.author: Every Frame a Painting
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>If there’s one question that every filmmaker asks themselves, it’s “Where do I put the camera?” Today, we consider the things that really matter when answering that question.

A Video Essay by Taylor Ramos & Tony Zhou
Music by Sean William
Additional Stock Music provided by Pond5
BTS footage filmed by Julie Ng

Links to some of the interviews
Martin Scorsese & Timothée Chalamet at GQ: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sB_JZLp521g
Guillermo del Toro at the DGA: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FjR5bT5YYU0
Greta Gerwig at the BFI London Film Fest: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=joUKN8ezVio

items.title: The Story of Nosferatu: A Symphony of Horror (1922)
items.author: Layton Eversaul
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>In this in-depth retrospective, I tell the story behind one of cinema's towering classics: 1922's "Nosferatu: A Symphony of Horror". I dive deep into its development and historical context, its production, release and reception, legal troubles and complex legacy. Directed by F.W. Murnau, with Costumes & Sets by Albin Grau. Starring Max Schreck, Gustav Von Wongenheim, Greta Schröder,  and Alexander Granach. 

NOW ON PATREON: https://www.patreon.com/LaytonEversaul
Twitter: https://twitter.com/LaytonEversaul
Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/LaytonReviews
Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/laytoneversaul</body></html>

items.title: When an Actor Risked Everything - Matthew McConaughey
items.author: FilmStack
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>The McConaissance: the brief period in Matthew McConaughey’s career where he completely reinvented himself, doing a full 180 from the romcoms that defined his previous decade. With groundbreaking performances in Dallas Buyers Club, Interstellar, and True Detective it taught us a valuable lesson: never underestimate what an actor is truly capable of.

But McConaughey’s sudden career shift didn’t just impress us; it gave everyone whiplash. How did an actor at the top of the romcom world risk everything by stepping away from such lucrative roles? And why, after achieving one of Hollywood’s greatest comebacks, did this golden period fizzle out so quickly? That’s what we’re exploring today. What led McConaughey to even attempt this transformation? How did he execute it so flawlessly? And what happened after the McConaissance ended? This is the story of an actor who just decided to reinvent himself. This is Matthew McConaughey.


Chapters:
00:00 - Intro
00:56 - Rise & Typecasting
05:32 - Why and how?
08:05 - The McConaissance
12:45 - Peak McConaissance
18:05 - How did it end?

Matthew McConaughey. The McConaissance. Dazed and Confused. The Wolf of Wall Street. Leonardo DiCaprio. Contact. The Wedding Planner. How to lose a guy in 10 days. Sahara. Tropic Thunder. The Lincoln Lawyer. Bernie. Killer Joe. Dallas Buyers Club. Magic Mike. Mud. Interstellar. Rust Cohle. True Detective. The Dark Tower. Sing. Kubo and the Two Strings. Fool's Gold. Best Modern Actor. Martin Scorcese. Christopher Nolan. 

If you like this video don't forget to leave a like, and if you're interested in videos about movies and the film industry in general, make sure to subscribe to FilmStack for more great content.

If you have any other ideas for videos, leave a comment and I might make a video with your idea.

Patreon: https://patreon.com/FilmStack
Twitter: https://twitter.com/realFilmStack
Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/realfilmstack/

#matthewmcconaughey     #dedication    #bestactor</body></html>

items.title: Best of the Worst: Christmas Wheel of the Worst (2024)
items.author: RedLetterMedia
items.content: <html><head></head><body>That time is here of the year!! Although, a little late to the gate it was this year. Christmas Best of the Worst can be tricky and this year’s episode was no exception. All hands on deck the halls! Mike, Jay, Rich, Tim, Josh, Jack, Josh, Jack, Jay, Tim, Josh, Jim, Josh, and Fartf*ck all join forces to watch three Christmas themed sh*t films. Usually we pick Christmas presents and unwrap them, but this year we did something a little different: Put wrapped Christmas presents on the Wheel of the Worst and spun it. Included with the gifts were lumps of Santa’s coal for the bad boys and boys. However, this year’s episode might go down as one of the worst episodes of anything ever, as our Christmas inventory is getting pretty low. Sure, we could have done a few non-Christmas movies but we wanted to keep it real this year. Rich was unusually grumpy. Mike was usually drunky. Ho ho ho.</body></html>

items.title: This Style Changed Cinema... And It's Back
items.author: Thomas Flight
items.content:
<html><head></head><body>Get your donation matched up to $100 at https://givewell.org 
- Choose YouTube - and enter Thomas Flight under "How Did You Hear About Us" (Valid through 2024 or while matching funds last)

Over 100 years ago a German style of filmmaking had a huge influence on cinema. Recently, with movies like The Substance, Expressionism has been making a comeback. 

Alt Title: "What Does The Substance Look Like That?"

This video avoids spoilers for The Substance.

References:
"Expressionism in the Cinema" by Olaf Brill; Gary D Rhodes
"Expressionism and Film" by Rudolf Kurtz
